result:
The first thing that arrives is not a sentence but a sound, the hush that lives between heartbeats when you realize the house is colder than it should be at 3:07 a.m. and the dog is staring at the hallway again. I’ve stopped believing that dogs see ghosts; I now believe they see time, the way it folds back on itself like a badly made bedsheet, corners tucked where none ought to be. My dog—her name is Thursday—doesn’t bark, she exhales, and the breath leaves her shaped like a question neither of us can pronounce.

I used to think 1000 words was a fence you could see across. Now it feels more like a tide: if I stand still, the words lap higher, tasting my ankles for weakness. Somewhere inside the count is the name of the boy I loved when I was fifteen and he smelled faintly of iodine because his father kept a glass flask of it on the bathroom shelf for “cuts, lies, and science.” I never asked which emergency required the largest dose. I only know that when he pressed his iodine mouth to mine I tasted rusted stars and the sudden knowledge that nothing would ever be that bright again. I have never been able to use the word “bittersweet” since; it feels like a souvenir from a country that never existed.

Thursday walks to the back door, nails ticking against oak, asking to be let out into a night that might already be morning if you squint. I open the door because refusal is a luxury I gave up the year my mother forgot my birthday but remembered the date of her first wedding to a man she met in a dream. Outside, the yard is a negative: white grass, black sky, the moon a hole someone punched to let the real light through. Thursday sniffs once, twice, gives me a look that says, You’re the one who wanted language, now live in it. She trots off, disappears between the fence slats, reappears inside the house again without moving. I accept this the way sailors accept weather: record, adjust, keep sailing.

There is a spoon on the counter with peanut butter still clinging to the bowl of it, shaped like a miniature cathedral. I lick it because wasting food is a sin against the future, and because the future feels like a thin sheet of ice I’ve already fallen through but haven’t yet noticed I’m wet. The peanut butter tastes of cellar dust and kindergarten glue, the edible paste of unsupervised afternoons. I remember that we were once told not to speak with our mouths full; nobody warned us that speaking with an empty mouth is even harder, because words need something to stick to.

I check the word count: 347. The number feels significant, like a street address I lived at in a life where all the clocks ran backwards and the mail arrived before it was written. I once wrote a letter to that address, addressed to myself, age nine, warning him not to climb the walnut tree on June 4 because the branch that looks trustworthy is the one that will teach him gravity’s indifference. I signed it “You, later,” then added a postscript: “P.S. The scar will fade, the belief that you deserved it won’t; work on that.” I never received an answer, unless you count the dream in which a small boy handed me a walnut still green in its husk, split open to reveal not a nut but a tiny folded map of the town I grew up in, every street renamed after apologies I still owe.

Thursday is now lying on the rug, head between paws, eyes open, watching the chimney like television. I follow her gaze and see, for the first time, that the bricks are not bricks but stacked calendars, each one showing a day I mistook for ordinary: a Tuesday when I drank tap water and it tasted like apples, a Friday when the bus driver waved me on though I had no fare, a Sunday when the rain fell in only one spot of the yard, watering a single dandelion that grew into a sun so convincingly a small planet formed around it, inhabited by creatures who worshipped thirst and called it paradise. I want to reach in and retrieve those days, spread them on the table like photographs, but the chimney flue is locked by a latch shaped like the word “nevertheless.”

I sit on the floor beside Thursday. The furnace hums a lullaby it learned from whales. I tell her the story of the boy who tried to count to infinity but stopped at 17 because that’s how many times his father said “I love you” the week before he left for good. Thursday listens by not listening, the way only dogs and midnight can. I scratch behind her ears and feel the centuries collapse into a single warm spot. She leans against my knee; the pressure translates into a sentence: You are forgiven for believing you had to finish every thought; some are meant to be culled like sick sheep so the flock can cross the mountains.

Word count now 712. I feel the ceiling lift slightly, as if the house exhaled. I remember that in some cultures the heart is counted twice: once as muscle, once as visitor. I try to thank mine for continuing to knock even when no one answers, like a Jehovah’s Witness of blood. It replies by speeding up, a Morse code message I translate as: keep going, the view from the next ribcage might surprise you.

I stand, open the fridge, not for food but for the small light that comes on like a rehearsal for death. Inside: a jar of pickles, a shriveled lime, a Tupperware of something once lasagna, now archaeology. I close the door quickly because the light is too sincere; it believes everything it illuminates deserves to exist. Instead I take the cordless phone from its cradle, though the service was disconnected years ago. I dial the number of my childhood home, a place now a parking lot beside a pharmacy. It rings once. A voice I almost recognize answers: “We’re not here, leave a message for who you were.” I say, “I’m sorry I packed your future in boxes labeled Miscellaneous.” I hang up first, the small victory of the living.

Thursday stands, stretches, walks to the front door. I understand it’s time. I fetch the leash though we both know it’s ceremonial; the only thing either of us is ever truly tethered to is the moment before the next word arrives. Outside, dawn is rehearsing its opening line, a pale sentence that begins with the color peach and ends with the sound of trash cans being lifted by invisible hands. We walk past the hedge where last summer a robin built a nest; the nest is still there, now a cathedral of straw without believers. Thursday sniffs it once, sneezes, moves on. I pause. Inside the hollow of straw I see four blue eggshell halves arranged into a compass pointing nowhere, or everywhere, depending on how willing you are to forget North.

We reach the corner where the streetlamp flickers its private stutter. I look up and see the bulb is not burning out but learning to speak; each flicker is a syllable of a language made of darkness almost touching light. I try to memorize the pattern before it resolves into ordinary malfunction. Thursday tugs gently; the leash slackens like a promise that has finished being made. I let her lead, because direction is a room we rent, not own.

Word count 967. The rest of the words are already walking toward us, barefoot, wearing robes stitched from the sound of Thursday’s paws receding. I could chase them, add them here like postage stamps on an envelope already addressed to the wind. Instead I stop, kneel, place my palm on the cooling pavement. It is not a prayer; it is a receipt. I have purchased this moment with the currency of every word I did not say when speaking would have required me to explain the taste of iodine to people who cover their wounds with silence.

When I stand, Thursday is a block ahead, a silhouette against the opening eye of sky. I do not call her back; I let the distance stretch like a sentence finally brave enough to end. The leash slips from my hand, falls in a loose spiral, a question mark lying sideways, asking nothing. I turn to go inside, counting not steps but heartbeats, each one a syllable closer to the final breath that will someday replace my name with a space just the right size for someone else’s thousand words.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
